passage:
A meteorologist analyzed average monthly precipitation (in millimeters) and wildfire incidents in a forested region over a single year.

| Month | Avg. Precipitation (mm) | Wildfire Incidents |
|---|---|---|
| January | 88 | 2 |
| February | 74 | 3 |
| March | 61 | 6 |
| April | 43 | 11 |
| May | 22 | 18 |
| June | 8 | 29 |
| July | 4 | 34 |
| August | 6 | 31 |
| September | 19 | 22 |
| October | 38 | 13 |
| November | 55 | 5 |
| December | 82 | 2 |

The meteorologist argued that lower monthly precipitation is strongly associated with higher wildfire incidence in this region.
question: Which observation from the data most directly supports the meteorologist's argument?

choices.A: July has the highest wildfire incidence at 34 incidents.
choices.B: The months with the lowest precipitation (June: 8 mm, July: 4 mm, August: 6 mm) correspond to the months with the highest wildfire incidence (29, 34, and 31 incidents), while the months with the highest precipitation (January: 88 mm, December: 82 mm) have the lowest wildfire incidence (2 incidents each).
choices.C: Wildfire incidents increase from January through July and then decrease.
choices.D: February has more precipitation than March and also fewer wildfire incidents.

explanation: The meteorologist's argument is a specific inverse relationship: lower precipitation is associated with higher wildfire incidence. The best support shows both ends of this relationship clearly. Choice B identifies both the dry, high-fire months (June, July, August) and the wet, low-fire months (January, December) together, directly illustrating the inverse relationship at both extremes. This double-ended comparison is the most complete support for the 'strong association' claim. Choice A notes only one data point (July's high incidence) without comparing it to precipitation. Choice C describes the temporal pattern (rise and fall) but doesn't explicitly link it to precipitation. Choice D makes a single-pair comparison that supports the trend but is far less comprehensive than Choice B.
